editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 19 [Bnf 12926 English Translation]
div.p: From these things it is shown clearly enough in what manner a life adorned with virtues in the preachers of the faith supplies the place of miracles, so that the unbelievers willingly hear the words of life and see reasonably that those things are to be believed, and thus at last they may gain them for Christ. On the contrary, however, if it should be infected with crimes, especially with devastations of lands, rapines, invasions, and unjust occupations of the dominions and possessions of those very unbelievers, and on account of this with innumerable of them killed and slaughtered, just as in the western Indian regions we have always and continuously done, that we have been an impediment to their efficacious conversion nothing can seem clearer.

¶ Wherefore we can gather that very many of the unbelievers are excused from the reception of the faith for a long time and perhaps for their whole life however long it may have lasted, as long as they see the most corrupt
and detestable morals of the Christians, even if with such men religious men go for the sake of preaching and preach in act. For they have sufficient impediments to believing, indeed to hearing and conversing with men of this kind, nay rather they have very many motives to the contrary, as appears from the sayings of Chrysostom.

For it is written Ecclesiasticus 34:
One builds up and another tears down, what does it profit them except labor?
And the labor will be far greater if one preaches and a thousand do the contrary. Scotus agrees in 4 Sententiarum distinction 3, question 4, where he says that no one is bound to any divine precept unless it is promulgated to him by someone suitable and authentic, or by truthful report and the testimony of good men, which anyone ought reasonably to believe. Which Scotus understands concerning a divine positive precept or law, or human, which is not known interiorly in the heart.

¶ But returning to the proposition, such unbelievers have no one suitable or authentic whom they ought reasonably to trust, nor is there among them truthful report, since they have not yet seen the preachers of the faith, as we suppose, nor the testimony of good men, since all of that kind are held by ignorance of unbelief. Therefore negative unbelievers will not always sin by worshiping idols. And consequently they will be punished illicitly on account of this,
that they worship idols, by human judgment. Concerning which in my judgment there ought to be no ambiguity to anyone through those things which have been alleged above.

objection ¶ There can also be adduced against the aforesaid the common opinion of all the holy doctors, namely that concerning those things which are of the faith and of divine law and necessary for salvation, invincible ignorance cannot fall. Because if they did what is in them, God is ready to illuminate them by His grace. Therefore if they are not illuminated, it is on account of their fault, because they do not do what is in them, and thus they sin. Therefore justly on account of that sin they can be punished even by human judgment.

The assumption is clear through
Saint Thomas especially in De Veritate, question 14, article 11, ad 1 , where speaking of a man raised among groves, forests, or wild beasts, he says thus:
For this pertains to divine providence, that it provide for each one concerning things necessary for salvation, provided he is not impeded on his part. For if someone should be nourished in the forests or among wolves, and followed the guidance of natural reason with the appetite for good and flight from evil, most certainly it is to be held that God would reveal through internal inspiration those things which are necessary to be believed, or would direct some preacher of the faith, just as he sent Peter to Cornelius, Acts 10.
These words of Saint Thomas; he teaches the same in Romans 10, lecture 3.

objection ¶ To this we respond first that not always to one doing what is in him is God ready to illuminate through His grace,
unless it is from the intention of God that he be illuminated and receive grace, and then someone is infallibly illuminated and receives grace. Thus teaches
Saint Thomas Prima Secundae, question 112, article 3 in the body
and ad 1, where he asks whether by necessity grace is given to one preparing himself for grace or doing what is in him.

And he responded that
preparation for grace can be considered in two ways: either according as it is from free will, and according to this it has no necessity to the obtaining of grace; or according as it is from God moving, and then it has necessity to that to which it is ordained by God, not indeed of coercion but of infallibility, because the intention of God cannot fail. Whence if it is from the intention of God moving that the man whose heart He moves should obtain grace, he infallibly obtains it,
and so forth. These words of Saint Thomas.

Therefore since for those who are foreknown and at last to be damned with the reprobate, it is not from the intention of God to impart grace or to illuminate them, however much they prepare themselves through free will or do what is in them, they will not obtain grace, and therefore then they do not sin by not receiving grace. Because not on account of a fault which is in them, since rather in them there is punishment than fault on account of original sin according to Augustine, where he will be alleged below, but because God does not will to illuminate them by His mere will, not on account of sins but because He does not will, from the fact that just as no cause of reprobation
or predestination is given on our part, so sometimes no cause of the effect itself of reprobation is given; but the effect of reprobation is to deny grace and the means necessary for the obtaining of grace.

But a means necessary for the obtaining of faith and grace is the exterior vocation itself through preachers, in which the predestination itself begins to be fulfilled according to Augustine, and similarly the interior vocation by which God calls by inspiring. Whence Saint Thomas in Quodlibet 2, article 6 says that
a man is bound to believe according to how he is aided to believe. But someone is aided to believe in three ways: first indeed through interior vocation, second through exterior teaching and preaching, third through exterior miracles.
These are his words.

Therefore if they are not aided by God through any of the aforesaid modes, as in the foreknown, they do not sin by not believing or not receiving grace. This Augustine intimates in the book On the Predestination of the Saints, chapters 9 and 10, and On the Gift of Perseverance, chapters 13 and 14, where among other profound and most beautiful things he says, speaking of the Tyrians and Sidonians, thus:
But the rest, where if not in the mass of perdition are they left by just divine judgment? Where the Tyrians were left and the Sidonians, who even could have believed if they had seen those miracles of Christ. But because it was not given to them to believe, even that from which they might believe was denied, namely preaching.
And below:
Therefore the eyes of the Tyrians and Sidonians were not so blinded,
nor was the heart so hardened, since they would have believed if they had seen such signs as these saw, namely the Jews of whom he also speaks there, but it did not profit even them that they could believe, because they were not predestined.
These words of Augustine.

¶ Therefore that common opinion of the doctors is understood, namely that concerning things necessary for salvation no one has invincible ignorance, because if he did what is in him, et cetera, concerning those who to eternal life through the mercy of God are preordained. For such persons, if they did what is in them, most certainly it is to be held that God would provide the remedy; but as often as they do not do it, they sin, as the saints say. And therefore these things do not obstruct what has been determined above.

¶ And he usurps divine judgment who judges uncertain and doubtful things.
2, q. 1, nomen presbyteria ; nor can that be condemned by human examination which God has reserved to His
judgment.
15, q. 6, c. si a sacerdotibus .

¶ Both because, as is taught more fully below, sins which are committed only between God and the sinner himself are reserved to divine judgment alone, as is clear in many things, especially in sins of thoughts, even if they were known to the otherwise competent judge to be most grave. And therefore no judge has the power of judging concerning hidden things.
The said c. erubescant, and 6, q. 1, c. si omnia , and the said c. consuluisti.

¶ Likewise it is clear in this, that the sin of the Jews and Saracens, even those subject to Christian princes, is not punished by human judgment but is reserved to divine judgment. Whence the canonists say in c. quod super his. de voto, that to be a Jew, and by the same reason a pagan or Saracen, is in itself a delict with respect to God and to eternal punishment, but not with respect to the judge and contentious forum, and thus it is not punishable by men. The same says Bartholus and others in l. apud Iulianum, § 1, and there Iasso § constat 5 columine. See the gloss in c. post miserabilem: de vsuris.

Since therefore the sin of those worshiping idols, even supposing that they are not excused from the sin of idolatry, is done only between God and the sinners themselves, and since it is very difficult, indeed impossible, to discern whom invincible ignorance does not excuse, and through this they are committed by most hidden cogitation, it follows that human judgment is excluded from the care of punishing
such sins, unless it wishes rashly to usurp divine judgment. Therefore the truth of the conclusion is clear, that namely the negative unbelievers would be punished illicitly on account of this alone, that they worship idols, at least by human judgment.

Against Innocent who said the pope can punish a people because they worship idols
And thus at last it appears sufficiently that Innocent in this matter errs enormously, when he says it pertains to the pope to punish a gentile having only the natural law if he worships idols. To his argument, namely because it is natural to worship one and only God the creator, we concede that it is natural, but after it shall have been known that there is a true and unique God. For then any rational creature will naturally understand that He is to be worshiped, and to Him every act of worship, reverence, and sacrifice is to be exhibited, et cetera.
